text: The surgeon uses traction and contraction movements, assisted by gas dissection, to progressively deepen into the retroperitoneal space. The external iliac artery and vein are identified, followed by the internal iliac artery and the ureter. A clip is placed on the uterine artery's origin. The same dissection is performed on the opposite side, cutting through fat tissue and following the external iliac artery and vein deeper into the retroperitoneal space, revealing the umbilical artery and uterine artery.

The surgeon opens the space below the uterine artery, identifying the pararectal fossa and clearly visualizing the umbilical and uterine arteries. A clip is applied to the uterine artery without damaging the ureter. The myomectomy begins with the myoma visibly compressing both tubal cornua. The surgeon starts dissection on the right side of the myoma using a ten millimeter forceps to apply cranial traction. The serosa is coagulated and cut, and the myoma is progressively extracted from its bed.

The surgeon uses forceps to tract the myoma cranially, revealing surrounding tissue that has been coagulated and cut. The procedure is vascular due to previous uterine artery control. The surgeon continues dissection by placing small bites of tissue inside the bipolar, coagulating and cutting each piece. The forceps are repositioned to the left side of the myoma, allowing visualization and control of the left aspect with minimal bleeding. The myoma is successfully removed.

The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation to achieve hemostasis, placing stitches with a fourteen millimeter needle and Vicryl suture two centimeters outside the uterine scar. This technique invaginates the tissue into the myoma bed for anatomical reconstruction. The intracorporeal knotting technique is employed to close the large myoma bed, ensuring a flat knot with assistance to lock it securely.

The surgeon places a series of flat knots every two centimeters using a 14mm needle with Vicryl suture, performing intracorporeal knotting to reconstruct the large myoma bed. The anatomical reconstruction appears successful, with the right proximal tubal cornea included in the closure, which is suitable for the patient's IVF program. The closure involves three or four stitches for the ten-centimeter myoma, ensuring a stable reconstruction.

The surgeon closes the knot, marking the end of the procedure. An electromechanical morcellator is placed in the left iliac fossa, operating at 700 volts per minute, to remove the myoma. The uterine pedicle is controlled, with the clip remaining in place, ensuring the uterine artery is well managed. The opposite side is checked, and the abdominal cavity is rinsed.
